data.title: Buy Nvidia Shares Ahead of GTC, Says Wells Fargo
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) stock might be showing little momentum right now but if history is anything to go by, that might all change soon.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The chip giant’s upcoming GTC 2026 event is scheduled for March 16‑19. Historically, the stock has tended to perform strongly after GTC, with Nvidia typically outperforming the SOX index by around 30% over the three months following the event, ranging from roughly 12% to 45% over the past few years.

Hence, says Wells Fargo analyst Aaron Rakers: “We’re NVDA buyers ahead of the event.”

Among the items Rakers will be looking out for are updates regarding the pipeline. With commitments already exceeding $500 billion and potentially stretching into calendar 2027, Rakers estimates Nvidia has recognized roughly $240 billion to $250 billion of that opportunity since Blackwell began shipping in late 2024. An update to $600 billion+ would be a positive.

Additionally, Rakers, who ranks among the top 1% of analysts on Wall Street, will be watching for updates on Nvidia’s move to the Kyber architecture – its next-generation, high-density rack design – and how lessons from the early GB200 NVL72 rollout might shape that transition. He’ll also be looking to see whether Feynman, Nvidia’s planned post-Rubin GPU architecture, represents a completely new approach or just an evolution of existing designs.

Rakers will also be keeping an eye on Rubin CPX, Nvidia’s GPU built for long‑context AI inference and expected to launch in late 2026, as well as how the company positions its Groq LPU for AI workloads. He’s looking for more detail on Rubin CPX’s architecture and how Nvidia plans to integrate Groq’s technology, particularly its large on-chip SRAM and streaming design that helps keep inference fast and low-latency.

There could be some “wildcards” thrown in the mix too. The 5-star analyst points out that AI infrastructure growth reflects Jevons Paradox – as compute gets more powerful, usage expands, driving more token generation. Within this context, Groq integration vs. new inference-focused silicon could be in focus. There has been speculation about whether Nvidia’s Groq IP agreement will lead to standalone chips or if the technology will be incorporated into the company’s future GPU roadmap.

Another wildcard focuses on the prospect of customization. AMD + Meta’s recent chiplet-based partnership highlights the potential for custom AI hardware for hyperscalers. Nvidia’s Blackwell and Rubin GPUs already use a chiplet/dielet architecture, with Vera-Rubin Ultra expanding this further. The Wall Street Journal has reported that Nvidia plans a dedicated inference chip for OpenAI, leveraging its Groq IP license to optimize AI inference. According to the report, OpenAI has committed to significant purchases, making customized AI hardware a potential wildcard for Nvidia’s competitive positioning.

All told, Rakers maintained an Overweight (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares, backed by a $265 price target. The implication for investors? Upside of 47% from current levels. (To watch Rakers’ track record, click here)

38 other analysts join Rakers in the bull camp, overpowering 1 Hold and Sell, each, and all adding up to a Strong Buy consensus rating. The forecast calls for one-year returns of 51%, considering the average target clocks in at $271.89. (See Nvidia stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Wells Fargo Says These 2 Beaten-Down Cybersecurity Stocks Could Rebound to $200
data.description:
Generative and agentic AI tech has proven to be a headwind for software stocks, and cybersecurity names have been affected too. That impact got more pronounced recently.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The negative catalyst here was Anthropic’s release of Claude Code Security, a new tool in the Claude AI model. It allows the AI to scan codebases – and also to tag vulnerabilities and inform the human users of potential patches. Cybersecurity stocks dropped sharply on the new tool’s release, based on the theory of ‘unload stocks first and sort out the disruption later.’

Even though the overall cybersecurity sector took a hit, and is down generally, the stock analysts at Wells Fargo see options in this beaten-down sector as ripe for a rebound. In fact, they’re predicting that two beaten-down cybersec shares will bounce back to $200 in the coming months.

Using the TipRanks platform, we can see that both of these Wells Fargo picks hold Strong Buy consensus ratings from the Street’s analysts. Let’s give them a closer look and find out just what Wells Fargo sees in them.

Zscaler (ZS)

We’ll start with Zscaler, a cloud-focused cybersec company that has been in operation since 2007. Zscaler’s approach to cybersecurity is based on ‘zero trust,’ an approach that treats everything as a potential threat and provides secure protection for both apps and data. The company makes extensive use of AI technology and leverages that to give real-time network security and extends that protection to IoT and OT. Zscaler’s Zero Trust platform is designed for enterprise use; users can set up unified security efforts without having to interlock various disparate security measures.

Better for the user, Zscaler’s platform is fully scalable and relevant to any private organization. The company’s customers can do away with separate network firewalls, using the one Zscaler system as overall protection. The platform allows for management and supervision of secured devices, classification of new devices, control over access and use, and recognition of incoming threats.

Zero Trust provides several advantages, including superior system protection, increased agility and productivity, and reduced complexity and costs. Zscaler boasts that it secures over 500 billion transactions every day and that some 40% of the Global 2000 companies use the platform.

In the company’s fiscal 2Q26 earnings release, Zscaler reported $815.8 million in revenue, a total that was up 26% year-over-year and was $16.8 million better than had been expected. The bottom line was $1.01 per share by non-GAAP calculations; this beat the forecast by 11 cents per share. The company realized free cash flow in the quarter of $169.1 million, up from $143.4 million year-over-year and reaching 21% of total revenue.

The shares still dropped following the readout as investors were expecting more upside and have been concerned about organic growth. Add in the worries about AI’s effect on the broader software segment and the shares are down by 31% year-to-date.

However, Wells Fargo analyst Richard Poland covers this stock, and he sees it as a sound pick at current prices. Poland writes, “We believe ZS has an entrenched position in the large enterprise space, already having captured 45% of the F500, 40% of the G2K and 20% of enterprises >2,000 employees… Ultimately, we believe new logos can continue to contribute $300-400M per year on ~700 new logos and view saturation concerns, even on a logo basis, as overstated… ZS trades at ~6x EV/ S & ~19x EV/FCF on our CY26 estimates, a discount to the peer group. We see a favorable risk/reward, as momentum is building across several growth pillars that we believe can drive upward estimate revisions in the coming years. If ZS can prove sustainable growth >20%, we expect the valuation gap to the peer group to close.”

Along with his Overweight (Buy) rating here, Poland sets a $200 price target that implies a one-year upside potential of 35% for the stock. (To watch Nowinski’s track record, click here.)

The Street’s consensus on ZS is a Strong Buy, based on 34 recent reviews that break down 30 to 4 in favor of Buy over Hold. Zscaler’s $154.67 trading price and $232.55 average price target indicate room for a gain of 50% in the coming 12 months. (See ZS stock forecast.)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: Archer Aviation Shares Crash Post-Earnings – Here’s What Needham Thinks Comes Next
data.description:
Archer Aviation (NYSE:ACHR) shares look set for a beating in Tuesday’s session following the company’s Q4 results. The readout offered a bigger-than-expected EPS loss partly driven by higher personnel and vendor-related costs. The company saw out FY25 with roughly $2.0 billion in liquidity and emphasized that it remains disciplined in managing its spending.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Since the eVTOL maker is still pre-revenue, it’s all about catalysts ahead, and looking forward to the rest of the year, Needham analyst Chris Pierce thinks the “bullish scenarios for ’26 are easily visible.”

The updated Midnight aircraft is progressing toward piloted transition flights, a key milestone in validating this new transportation tech – something ACHR’s Midnight was unable to demonstrate in 2025, thereby “dampening investor enthusiasm.” Succeeding here could pave the way for a shift in sentiment, while flights under the FAA’s eVTOL eIPP (Integration Pilot Program) should further boost investor confidence ahead of the LA Summer Olympics in 2028. There, ACHR will serve as the official air taxi provider, and the event should represent a “full coming out party for the industry, with certification potentially in the rearview.”

Additionally, ACHR’s $2 billion cash balance underscores its position as a long-term industry leader, providing the flexibility to navigate potential delays, whether company-specific or affecting the broader sector. The company’s spending so far highlights how critical funding is to getting an eVTOL player operational. “We see an industry coalescing around a handful of leaders, with cash and cash burn lowering the threat of new entrants,” Pierce went on to say.

Yet the analyst concedes that all of that is unlikely to convince ACHR bears, who will recall having heard all this before. ACHR previously indicated on its 1Q25 earnings call last May that piloted flight would occur “in the coming days,” and while the company eventually delivered, it was a piloted CTOL (conventional takeoff and landing) flight, which fell short of investor expectations for a piloted eVTOL transition flight. Now, according to the language in the Q4 shareholder letter, ACHR is targeting piloted transition flight “in the coming months,” which could serve as a “bullish catalyst.” However, Pierce anticipates “elevated skepticism” from the bears regarding this claim.

Pierce’s updated model pushes back aircraft production amid higher financial losses, which raises the company’s capital needs. He now expects ACHR to raise $500 million across FY26 and FY27, leading to greater dilution than previously anticipated.

Accordingly, the analyst lowered his price target from $10 to $9, suggesting the stock will gain 34% in the months ahead. Pierce’s rating stays a Buy. (To watch Pierce’s track record, click here)

2 other analysts join Pierce in the bull camp, while an additional 2 Holds add up to a Moderate Buy consensus rating. There are ample gains projected here; at $13.33, the average target implies shares will climb by 98% over the one-year timeframe. (See ACHR stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Middle East War Merits a Price Target Hike for Palantir Stock, Says Rosenblatt
data.description:
War, what is it good for? Well, in Palantir’s (NASDAQ:PLTR) case it appears to be good enough for a new price target.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

At least in the opinion of Rosenblatt analyst John McPeake. “War regrettably underscores the value of Palantir over just another LLM,” said the analyst, raising his price target from $150 to $200, now suggesting the shares will gain 41% over the coming months. Naturally, McPeake’s rating stays a Buy. (To watch McPeake’s track record, click here)

Over the past few days, the situation evolved rapidly, with several developments unfolding in quick succession. On Feb 26, Anthropic stated that its models should not be deployed in fully autonomous weapons systems. A day later, on Feb 27, the Trump administration directed all U.S. government agencies to cease using Anthropic’s AI technologies. Then, at 09:45 Tehran time (01:15 ET) on Feb 28, Operation Epic Fury began.

The U.S. government has mandated a six-month phase-out of Anthropic’s large language models, which McPeake thinks provides “ample time to switch over to one of the many other LLMs that are supported by Palantir.” The analyst believes heightened global instability and the growing demand for “comprehensive wartime solutions” could drive additional agreements similar to the July U.S. Army deal that consolidated 75 separate contracts into a single arrangement with Palantir. McPeake also views data analysis as the primary LLM performance requirement in theater-of-war settings, and in that respect, he thinks there are “adequate alternatives” for Anthropic’s Claude.

No Palantir discussion can be concluded without a look at its famous valuation. McPeake’s initial $150 price target was based on a 0.9x PEG multiple applied to his Street-high 2027 estimates, with the new price target raising the PEG estimate to 1.2x (88x 2027 EPS). Here, McPeake believes a “premium valuation is more than warranted.”

When comparing Palantir’s revenue trajectory with that of the most successful tech companies at a similar stage, McPeake points out that its projected growth stands out. Based on its 2026 guide, Palantir is expected to grow faster in year 8 than any of the major long-term value creators did at a comparable size. The analyst also thinks it is worth noting that some of those companies – such as Facebook and Google at the time – benefited from transformative acquisitions, while Palantir’s expansion to date has been entirely organic.

So, that’s the Rosenblatt view, but what does the rest of the Street make of Palantir’s prospects? With an additional 13 Buys, 4 Holds and 2 Sells, the analyst consensus rates the stock a Moderate Buy. At $191.76, the average target offers 12-month upside of 35%. (See Palantir stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘Sell-Off Is Overdone,’ Says RBC About AeroVironment Stock
data.description:
AeroVironment (NASDAQ:AVAV) was among a group of defense stocks to surge in early Monday’s trading following the military escalation between the US/Israel and Iran but those gains quickly evaporated with the stock seeing out the session 17% into the red.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The reversal came after a Space News report indicated that the DoW plans to open the SCAR (Satellite Communications Augmentation Resource) program to new bids, aiming to diversify suppliers and potentially reduce costs. As a result, the drone maker could potentially lose its roughly $1.4 billion contract with the U.S. Space Force. That contract was originally awarded to BlueHalo, an AeroVironment subsidiary acquired last year.

RBC’s Kenneth Herbert, an analyst ranked among the top 1% on Wall Street, concedes that the SCAR program has “hurt credibility,” but he thinks the reaction was excessive.

Nevertheless, Herbert admits the “outlook for the SCAR program is much more in doubt.” That said, the company still anticipates successfully renegotiating at least part of the program by around May, which he thinks would be “incrementally positive” for the stock. Herbert currently reckons that the fiscal year 2026 revenue at risk due to the stop work order is roughly $100 million, based on a full-year run rate of about $140 million, since the program was weighted toward the second half of the year. The analyst expects the company to revise its FY26 full-year guidance to account for the absence of second-half SCAR program revenues. Additionally, a substantial portion of the approximately $1.4 billion in unfunded backlog tied to the program is likely to be removed. However, Herbert stays onside here. “We remain bullish on AVAV considering the unmanned opportunity, and believe the core of the business remains sound,” the 5-star analyst went on to add.

Still, Herbert thinks management should take a more proactive approach in shaping the narrative around the SCAR program. The ambiguity surrounding “backlog definition” in the fiscal second-quarter 2026 results contributed to “investor uncertainty,” and recent developments in the program have reinforced the perception that management may not have full visibility into the business. Herbert thinks the stock would benefit from a pre-announcement ahead of the fiscal third-quarter 2026 results next week (March 10), giving AVAV the opportunity to clarify the SCAR program’s impact on backlog, the balance sheet, and 2H26 financial results. “We believe this would provide the framework for a more constructive discussion on the outlook and potential sources of upside,” Herbert summed up.

To this end, Herbert maintained an Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares although his price target goes from $375 to $325. Nevertheless, there’s still upside of 56% from current levels. (To watch Herbert’s track record, click here)

Most analysts agree with that take; the stock claims a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on a mix of 14 Buys and 1 Hold and Sell, each. At $369.50, the average target implies shares will climb by 77% in the months ahead. (See AVAV stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Buy AMD Shares Ahead of 2H26 Ramp, Says UBS
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) has already improved its AI chip positioning considerably after nabbing deals with some of the leading AI companies, but the semi giant looks poised to add another string to its bow.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That is the conclusion reached by UBS’s Timothy Arcuri, an analyst ranked in 5th spot among the thousands of Street experts, following a semiconductor bus tour – i.e., a meeting with company representatives. “We emerged more confident in revenue upside for C2027 as the company is now suggesting there could be a third GW-scale customer beyond the deals it has struck with OpenAI and META. While we can’t be sure who this is, MSFT seems to us the most likely,” said the 5-star analyst.

The conversation also touched on investor concerns that the warrant structure and 10% equity stakes granted to Meta and OpenAI could become standard requirements for gigawatt-scale partnerships, with AMD firmly rejecting that notion. The company characterized those arrangements as “unique deals” designed specifically for large language model developers, given the degree of control they exert over the broader ecosystem, and emphasized that it intends to be highly selective and does not plan to extend similar structures to all customers. AMD further argued that the structure should be accretive over the long term and sees it as a mechanism to significantly speed up its market share expansion across the wider accelerator market.

The company also believes the long-term operating margin of its GPU business is closer to 35%, rather than 25%, and sees the first two big deals as scale-building initiatives that enhance long-term economic value. The Meta warrants, similar to the OpenAI warrants, also include additional technical provisions that require the customer to ready its software stack, rather than simply accept product shipments.

Elsewhere, AMD was “very bullish” on the CPU business, noting that Server CPU market growth has exceeded expectations. The November Analyst Day projections were worked on in summer 2025, and since then demand indicators have “continued to strengthen.” As a result, AMD now sees potential upside to its previously outlined $60 billion 2030 market opportunity and 18% five-year CAGR, with growth supported by both higher unit volumes and stronger ASPs.

The company expects CPU demand to benefit from AI-related workloads as well as traditional enterprise applications, alongside an improving pricing backdrop. Historically, AMD assumed that rising core counts would be offset by declining ASP per core, but it now anticipates ASP per core to remain broadly flat rather than trending downward.

While Arcuri came away confident in AMD’s prospects, he thinks it is “more of a 2H:26 story,” as MI450 shipments tied to the OpenAI deal begin, with Meta-related shipments expected to follow shortly thereafter.

Bottom line, Arcuri maintained a Buy rating on the shares, backed by a $310 price target that points toward 12-month returns of 56%. (To watch Arcuri’s track record, click here)

22 other analysts are also AMD bulls, while 8 Holds add up to a Moderate Buy consensus rating. The forecast calls for one-year returns of 44%, considering the average target clocks in at $286.04. (See AMD stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Goldman Sachs Names 2 Top HALO Stocks for Its March Conviction List
data.description:
HALO stocks are starting to attract more attention. These stocks feature a fundamentally sound profile of ‘heavy asset, low obsolescence.’ It’s a profile defined by a combination of high levels of physical capital and lasting economic relevance. HALO stocks can be found in the energy sector, in transport infrastructure, and in heavy industrial equipment.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Now, some of Wall Street’s top firms and analysts are starting to turn to halos as conviction names for the near future.

Steven Kron, Goldman Sachs’ Managing Director and Director of Americas Equity Research, is leaning into the halos, writing in a recent note, “Investors are finally giving more value to old world capital intensive businesses that is now extending beyond just those investing in hard data center assets to other slices of the supply chain and the economy. Why? The old adage that you buy the shovel makers in a gold rush is at least part of the answer, of course. And as strategist Peter Oppenheimer puts it: for the first time since the internet’s commercialization a quarter-century ago, future technology growth prospects are increasingly dependent on physical assets like data centers and energy supplies. Drilling down on this opportunity, we identify the best HALO businesses that pair substantial physical capital (barriers to replication via cost, regulation, time to build or engineering complexity) with long-lived economic relevance.”

Kron’s colleagues at Goldman are busy picking out halos to buy for the near term and have added a pair to the firm’s conviction list. We’ve opened the TipRanks database to see what the rest of the Street makes of their choices. Let’s take a closer look.

Loar Holdings, Inc. (LOAR)

The first HALO stock on our radar, Loar Holdings, is the parent company to a family of subsidiaries, firms that act in aerospace and defense niches. Through its subsidiaries, Loar produces a wide variety of aircraft and defense industrial components; these include airframe, structural, and braking system components; avionics; composites; de-icing equipment; electro-mechanical and engineered materials; flight controls and fluid-motion controls; restraints and safety devices; and human-machine interface devices.

Loar’s subsidiary firms bring long experience to the field: some have been in operation for 25 years, others for 60 to 70 years or more. Overall, the company has a diverse base of manufacturing skills and capabilities, along with high-end engineering and product testing services. All of these give solutions to the aerospace/defense sector, based on precision performance specifications.

We should note that, in January of this year, Loar completed its most recent merger transaction, the $250 million acquisition of Harper Engineering. Harper has two facilities in the Seattle area, and Loar expects a $30 million tax benefit from the transaction.

Loar’s financial results in 4Q25, the last period reported, were sound. The company’s revenue of $131.8 million was up 19% year-over-year and beat the forecast by $3.75 million. The company realized a 26-cent non-GAAP EPS, 7 cents per share better than had been anticipated.

Goldman analyst Noah Poponak is impressed by this company’s overall status as a first-rate industrial player. He writes, “LOAR is one of the highest quality Aerospace & Defense ‘earnings compounders,’ with very strong margins and free cash conversion, and still in the early stages of long-term growth and margin expansion. With revenues roughly twenty times smaller than competitor TDG, LOAR has ample balance sheet capacity, and is a preferred buyer in a very fragmented aerospace supply chain — providing the company with a long runway to acquire assets and supplement strong organic growth. All of this comes at a valuation discount to the average A&D supplier peers.”

The Goldman expert puts a Buy rating here, along with a $98 price target that implies an upside of 35.5% in the coming year. (To watch Poponak’s track record, click here.)

LOAR shares have 3 recent analyst reviews on record, and all are positive for a unanimous Strong Buy consensus rating. The stock is priced at $72.30, and the $87.67 average price target indicates room for a 21% gain in the months ahead. (See LOAR stock forecast.)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: “Unprecedented Demand Coming,” Says Needham About Red Cat Stock
data.description:
Defence stocks are drawing attention in Monday’s trading, with several names climbing sharply following the latest military flare-up in the Middle East involving the US/Israel and Iran.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Red Cat Holdings (NASDAQ:RCAT) is among the notable gainers, with shares of the Puerto Rico-based drone technology firm up 20% as of writing, reflecting investor interest in the prospect of higher defense spending and additional contract awards for companies operating in this space.

Red Cat stand to benefit here, and according to Needham analyst Austin Bohlig, the company is well-positioned to make the most of any upcoming opportunities.

The company hosted its first Innovation Day last week at its Blue Ops facility in West Palm Beach, Florida, with attendees getting a closer look at its expanding capabilities. Bohlig came away singing the company’s praises. “We walked away meaningfully more impressed with the multi-domain unmanned operating platform that Red Cat is building following its Innovation Day,” the analyst said.

The highlight was the coordinated live exercise featuring the latest USV (unmanned surface vehicle) Variant 7 working alongside multiple Black Widow systems in a simulated tactical scenario. Bohlig believes the demonstration clearly represented the company’s “transition from a single-platform drone manufacturer to a legitimate multi-domain unmanned systems provider with the formal launch of its Variant 7 USV.”

Beyond the technology showcase, Bohlig is convinced 2026 could mark a period of “significant growth,” supported by more than $2 billion in direct DoW program funding available across the USV and UAS portfolio. Bohlig’s current 2026 revenue forecast of $100 million remains below the Street’s estimate of ~$143 million, as he assumes that several large-scale programs will require time to scale. That said, with roughly $2 billion in funding opportunities now in play, he believes there is a realistic path for the guide to move beyond his present forecast and approach consensus expectations, with additional upside dependent on execution and the timing of contract awards over the course of the year.

Bohlig also continues to view RCAT’s manufacturing capacity buildout favorably, as it appears positioned to support what he expects will be an “unprecedented demand environment” in the upcoming 12–18 months.

“In our view,” Bohlig summed up, “companies that can ramp production quickly and reliably will ultimately capture outsized share as demand accelerates. Based on what we saw, Red Cat appears to be positioning itself to compete on throughput, not just technology.”

To this end, Bohlig reiterated a Buy rating on the shares, while his $16 price target points toward 12-month returns of 14%. (To watch Bohlig’s track record, click here)

Only 2 other analysts have been tracking RCAT’s progress, but both are also positive, making the consensus view a Strong Buy. The forecast calls for one-year gains of 26%, considering the average target clocks in at $17.67. (See RCAT stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘Shortages Will Last a While Yet’ – Timothy Arcuri Raises Micron Stock’s Price Target
data.description:
Micron (NASDAQ:MU) has been benefitting from elevated AI-driven demand for memory products as the market has been suffering from a sharp lack of supply. That has driven memory prices higher. The good news for the company is that it looks like the shortages could last a while yet.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That is the conclusion reached by UBS’s Timothy Arcuri, an analyst ranked in 4th spot among the thousands of Street stock experts, who says his latest round of industry checks “again points to strengthening pricing dynamics across both core DRAM and NAND.”

“Our view is that shortages could now last through C2027 and even into C2028 – especially for DRAM,” the 5-star analyst went on to say.

With GPU demand continuing to rise, Arcuri believes memory manufacturers are struggling to expand capacity quickly enough due to limited fab space, long equipment lead times, and a shortage of field service and process engineers to install and qualify new tools. As a result, almost all new capacity is being directed toward HBM, which is worsening the already tight supply of DDR5 and NAND.

The analyst now forecasts Micron’s EPS will reach around $60 in calendar 2026 (some distance above the Street at ~$40) and near $85 in 2027 (Street has ~$48). Arcuri also thinks Micron is aiming to capitalize on current market constraints by securing long-term agreements that might limit some near-term pricing gains but would support more stable revenue and earnings growth in the coming years.

On a separate note, Arcuri also shared key insights from a UBS bus tour meeting, which centered on the increasing importance of memory – especially DRAM – as a “key differentiator” in AI hardware and a driver for hyperscalers to sustain sharply falling token costs (for example, GOOGL’s Gemini serving unit costs are down more than 78% year-over-year). With this in mind, Micron strongly rejected claims that it is lagging peers on HBM4 technology or qualification timelines, emphasizing that it has already started shipping HBM4 about a quarter ahead of schedule and dismissing the idea that it will rely on HBM3e longer due to qualification delays at Nvidia. Regarding long-term agreements, the company offered limited details, noting mainly that customers seek a consistent and strong innovation roadmap on an annual basis. With tens of billions of dollars in capex underway, Micron is “trying to minimize volatility in its business and the risk from the capex deployment.”

Bottom line, Arcuri maintained a Buy rating on MU shares and raised his price target from $450 to $475, suggesting the stock will gain 16% in the months ahead. (To watch Arcuri’s track record, click here)

While one of Arcuri’s colleagues remains on the sidelines here, all 26 others are also positive, making the consensus view a Strong Buy. However, going by the $413 average target, shares will stay rangebound for the time being. As such, watch out for further price target hikes or rating downgrades shortly. (See Micron stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘A Top Pick Once Again,’ Says Morgan Stanley About Nvidia Stock
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) is getting little love from the market right now, even though investors could have hardly asked for a better performance from the chip giant in its FQ4 earnings report.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

It was casually excellent on all fronts once again and featured the kind of all-round beats investors have become accustomed to. The stock, however, subsequently dropped and has pulled back by 10% over the past two sessions. In fact, as Morgan Stanley analyst Joseph Moore points out, the shares have been unable to generate any momentum in recent months. “For the last two quarters NVIDIA has not moved while business has continued to strengthen – a function of concerns about the durability of current growth. Those concerns should turn to 2027 enthusiasm in the coming months,” Moore, who ranks among the top 2% of Street stock experts, explained.

The bear case is that hyperscalers’ negative cash flow could limit future growth. That’s not entirely unfounded – some cloud infrastructure businesses do run at a loss – but strong balance sheets and the ability to raise cash help cushion the risk. Spending might slow a bit next year, yet cloud companies are likely to make more from these investments than most forecasts expect, especially with profitable cloud GPU businesses and growing supply-demand gaps. Meanwhile, frontier model developers want far more compute than the market can actually provide, thanks to semiconductor limits, while global supply chains show companies are paying upfront to secure growth, with no sign of a 2026 peak. “So we don’t buy into hundreds of gigawatts in 2029,” says Moore, “but we also don’t see a peak in 2026, and our growth expectations for CY27 grow seemingly by the week.”

Moore points out that over the past three years, there has consistently been early-year skepticism about the year ahead. Yet each time, as clarity emerged and the underlying strength became apparent, NVDA stock experienced periods of strong outperformance. “Some year those concerns may be right, but our conviction is pretty high that this pattern will repeat,” the analyst added.

Another concern revolves around eroding market share. The analyst does expect Nvidia’s peers to grow a bit faster in CY26, largely because it’s harder for Nvidia to sustain rapid growth when approaching $80 billion per quarter amid supply constraints. As a result, Nvidia’s market share could slip slightly, maybe by a point or two this year.

It’s also true the moats have weakened somewhat as frontier model developers take an architecturally flexible approach, using Nvidia, AMD, TPUs, and internal ASICs. Still, Nvidia remains preferred in many cases, and Moore’s checks suggest the largest ASIC and AMD users are each likely to grow their Nvidia-based business by over 80% in CY26. “Market share concerns will be addressed at the coming GTC launch (March 16–19),” says the analyst, “demonstrating a leadership roadmap that again puts NVIDIA ahead of competition.”

With Moore claiming the stock now offers a “surprisingly good entry point,” it is once again a “top pick” in semis (taking Micron’s place). To this end, Moore maintained an Overweight (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares backed by a $260 price target, a figure that points toward one-year gains of 47%. (To watch Moore’s track record, click here)

Elsewhere on the Street, the stock claims an additional 36 Buys and 1 Hold and Sell, each, all for a Strong Buy consensus rating. Going by the $273.38 average target, a year from now, shares will be changing hands for a 54% premium. (See Nvidia stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Michael Burry Slams CoreWeave Stock but This Analyst Says to Buy the Dip
data.description:
CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) shares’ early Monday performance appears to be continue last week’s sharp pullback.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The stock fell by 18.5% on Friday following the release of the company’s Q4 results. The mixed earnings report featured strength in revenue and backlog, but was offset by near-term margin pressure due to infrastructure capacity buildouts.

The decline continued at the start of the week as a prominent market watcher issuing a warning on the company.

“The Big Short” investor Michael Burry cautioned that the AI infrastructure provider bears similarities to prior tech-bubble-era stocks. Burry argued that CoreWeave operates effectively as an off-balance-sheet special purpose vehicle structured to generate losses, but that elevated enthusiasm around AI-related spending ultimately drove it to go public. He said the situation echoes Level 3 Communications during the last major tech bubble, while pointing out that CoreWeave could face deeper challenges because Level 3’s infrastructure assets did not depreciate nearly as quickly, in his view making CoreWeave’s model comparatively more vulnerable.

CEO Mike Intrator has argued that the company’s ongoing investment phase is warranted, emphasizing that demand from enterprise and sovereign AI customers remains robust.

That view is echoed by Roth analyst Rohit Kulkarni, who, in post-earnings comments, recommended investors “buy AI cloud leader on dips.”

Kulkarni sees OpenAI’s recent $110 billion fundraising as “supportive of medium-term insurance” for CoreWeave’s contracted capacity model, although the softer margin guide for 2026 could weigh on near-term share performance. If CoreWeave can show that it is moving toward an investment-grade credit rating – especially through the upcoming delayed-draw loan (DDTL) tied to its contracts with Meta Platforms – that would likely boost confidence and could be a “meaningful positive catalyst.”

There could be another catalyst on the way in the shape of Nvidia’s GTC event (March 16–19), which will provide further clarity on GPU demand, Rubin deployments, and possibly insights into next-generation architectures. CoreWeave has indicated it plans to kick off delivery of Rubins in the second half of 2026, positioning itself among the first to market. “We think the deepening partnership between CRWV and NVDA continues to be an undervalued asset with potential access to lower cost of capital as the company looks to fund $30-35bn of CapEx in 2026,” the 5-star analyst went on to say.

On a “neutral-to-supportive AI sentiment,” at current levels, Kulkarni believes CRWV shares present a “highly asymmetric risk/reward.”

Accordingly, Kulkarni maintained a Buy rating on the shares backed by a $110 price target. The implication for investors? Upside of 38% from current levels. (To watch Kulkarni’s track record, click here)

The Street’s average target is even a little higher; at $114.18, the figure presents 12-month upside of 43.5%. On the rating front, based on a mix of 11 Buys and 8 Holds, the stock claims a Moderate Buy consensus rating. (See CRWV stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Oil Prices Set to Spike After US-Israel Strikes on Iran – 2 Energy Stocks Positioned to Gain
data.description:
Everything old is new again – by which we mean that open war has broken out again in the Middle East. The world woke up early Saturday to the news – that US and Israeli forces had begun a comprehensive attack on Iran’s nuclear weapons program, its government, and its military.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The political and military effects and ripple effects of this attack are beyond the scope of our analysis here – but at least one first-order market effect is fairly obvious: the price of oil is going to spike.

Iran’s southern coast lies along the vital Strait of Hormuz, one of the most important arteries of the global energy trade. About 20% of the world’s supplies of oil pass through the Strait, along with 20% of the world’s seaborne natural gas traffic. The worry is that the war can spill over and strike the tankers – and in fact, on Sunday, Reuters reported that a tanker was on fire in the Strait. Just as worrisome, some 150 tankers – with cargoes of crude oil, oil products, and liquefied natural gas – were reported to have dropped anchor across the Middle East.

With oil prices surging early on Monday, the impact on energy stocks will be immediate. An oil price spike can easily translate into higher profits and earnings – and we’ve used the TipRanks data platform to look up two energy stocks that are positioned to gain from this. The data shows that both stocks have Strong Buy consensus ratings, so let’s give them a closer look.

Diamondback Energy (FANG)

The first company on our list, Diamondback Energy, is a Texas-based operator in the US hydrocarbon exploration and production (E&P) sector. The company has been in operation for almost 20 years, since 2007, and has extensive land holdings in the rich Permian Basin of West Texas – the geological formation that has made Texas a major force in the world energy markets in the 21st century.

This pure-play Permian footprint puts Diamondback in a sound position to reap gains if global oil prices surge. First, the company will gain directly from selling its product at a higher price. Second, Diamondback’s production comes from regions that are not impacted by the war and are close to Gulf Coast export points.

Getting to specifics, Diamondback operates on land holdings in the Wolfcamp, Spraberry, and Bone Spring formations of the Permian. The company focuses on the acquisition, development, exploration, and exploitation of unconventional oil and natural gas resources in these areas, and the specific drilling operations include horizontal exploitation of multiple intervals. Diamondback makes extensive use of hydraulic fracturing to enhance production, and in Q4 of this year, the company averaged an output of 969.1 MBOE/d.

Diamondback last released financial results for 4Q25; in that quarter, the company generated total revenue of $3.38 billion. This figure was down 9% year-over-year, but it beat the forecast by over $130 million. At the bottom line, Diamondback’s earnings per share of $1.74 – the non-GAAP figure – came in 26 cents per share below expectations. For the quarter, the company reported an adjusted free cash flow of $1.2 billion.

In the quarterly report, Diamondback declared a $1.05 common share dividend payment, with a pay date of March 12. This represented a 5% increase in the dividend from the previous quarter, and the $4.20 annualized rate gives a forward yield of 2.4%.

For Mizuho analyst Nitin Kumar, Diamondback Energy presents a sound investment profile; as he explains,  “The company has always maintained a focus on keeping capital and operating costs low, which help improve cash margins. This is supported by a strong balance sheet and allows the company to be a leader in cash return payouts, with 50%+ of free cash flow to be returned to investors, including a high base dividend yield, opportunistic buybacks and ‘make-whole’ dividends. A deal with the Stephens family to buy up to ~3mm shares per quarter also gives some clarity on the pace of a potential sell-down by the foundation. We believe the reserve quality and depth, combined with operational and financial execution track record create a ‘must own’ stock in the independent E&P subsector…”

These comments back up Kumar’s Outperform (Buy) rating, while his $205 price target implies a one-year upside potential of 18%. (To watch Kumar’s track record, click here.)

That’s a higher upside than the average here. FANG shares have a $174.08 trading price and a $183.75 average target price; together, these indicate a 5.5% gain in the next 12 months. The stock has 21 recent analyst reviews, and the 19 to 2 split in favor of Buy over Hold easily supports the Strong Buy consensus rating. (See FANG stock forecast.)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: Applied Optoelectronics Stock Surges by 45% – Here’s Why It Remains a ‘Top Pick’ for This 5-Star Analyst
data.description:
Applied Optoelectronics (NASDAQ:AAOI) investors will be heading off to the weekend in high spirits with the shares surging by 45% in Friday’s session at the time of writing.  

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The surge came off the back of the networking products specialist’s 4Q25 results, which handily beat Street expectations. Revenue came in at $134.27 million, up 33.9% year-over-year, exceeding the analysts’ forecast by $6.03 million. Adj. EPS reached -$0.01, ahead of the estimates by $0.10.

For the first quarter of 2026, the company expects revenue in the range of $150 million to $165 million, compared with the $145.72 million consensus estimate. The company also raised its 2026 revenue outlook to above $1 billion, vs. the Street at $834 million, and lifted its 2026 operating margin forecast to 12%, compared with the 8% consensus estimate. Additionally, AAOI outlined a pathway to approximately $4 billion in revenue in 2027, including explicit projections for $378 million per month in Data Center revenue by the second half of 2027.

Rosenblatt’s Mike Genovese, an analyst ranked among the top 2% on Wall Street, hailed the “better-than-expected” results and guidance despite a one-quarter delay in the ramp of 800G revenue (high-speed optical transceivers transmitting 800 gigabits per second), now expected in Q2. The delay was attributed to a firmware customization issue with Amazon that is expected to be fully resolved by mid-March.

The company allocated $209 million to capital expenditures in 2025, primarily to expand laser and transceiver production capacity in Texas. Genovese believes this sustained investment should support approximately a threefold increase in Data Center revenue in 2026, with an even stronger growth trajectory anticipated in 2027.

Demand for 800G products from Amazon and Oracle is set to be the main revenue contributor in 2026, complemented by Microsoft’s steady 100G demand and recently renewed 400G demand. By 2027, each of these customers is also expected to purchase 1.6T products.

Beyond its existing customer base, AAOI is also working with additional prospective large-scale clients, including a promising new transceiver opportunity and four highly engaged UHP laser/CPO prospects.

Given the results, the 5-star analyst has now raised his 2026 revenue and EPS forecasts to $1.02 billion (from $805 million) and $1.18 (from $1.01), respectively, and has introduced 2027 estimates of $3.3 billion in revenue and $6.25 in EPS.

All told, AAOI retains its Top Pick status with Genovese reiterating a Buy rating and raising his price target from $50 to $125. The figure suggests share gains of 60% over the next 12 months. (To watch Genovese’s track record, click here)

1 other analyst joins Genovese in the bull camp, while 2 additional Holds add up to a Moderate Buy consensus rating. Going by the $78.5 average target, shares will stay rangebound for the time being. (See AAOI stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘No Warner Bros., No Problem,’ Says Cowen About Netflix Stock Following Bid Withdrawal
data.description:
Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) investors were evidently not too pleased with the pursuit of a Warner Bros. acquisition. Shares are surging in Friday’s session after the streaming giant decided to walk back on its plans to buy WBD’s studio and streaming assets.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The drawn-out saga recently led to Paramount increasing its bid to purchase all of WBD to $31 per share in cash, up from $30. This higher bid has surpassed WBD’s prior deal with Netflix, which valued the company’s studio and streaming businesses at $27.75 per share. The Warner Bros. Discovery board said it considered Paramount’s $31-per-share offer superior to its existing agreement with Netflix and that the streaming giant had four business days to revise its own offer in response to the higher bid.

The company has chosen not to match Paramount’s offer, stating that, following Paramount’s revised bid, the deal price is “no longer financially attractive.”

Cowen analyst John Blackledge said he found Netflix’ decision to walk away as “somewhat surprising,” but as has been the case, he thought investors would probably react positively to the news.

By declining to match Paramount’s bid, Netflix is effectively ending its pursuit of the storied Warner Bros. film and television studio, whose library includes titles such as Casablanca, The Matrix, Friends, Harry Potter, and The Lord of the Rings, along with major intellectual property like DC and Looney Tunes, and a significant theatrical distribution operation. The company is also walking away from HBO Max, which could have formed a formidable streaming combination. That said, Blackledge does not view the asset as essential for Netflix to continue expanding and maintaining its leadership in the global longform streaming market.

While Blackledge actually expected Netflix to match Paramount’s latest offer, management’s reasoning was clearly outlined in stating that the transaction no longer made financial sense. Netflix characterized Warner Bros. as a “nice to have” rather than a “must have at any price,” a view Blackledge shares. The decision also spares Netflix from integrating what would likely have been a highly complex theatrical business, though it still has ownership of select theaters, including the historic Paris Theater in New York and the Egyptian Theatre in Hollywood, which it uses for premieres and limited runs.

Netflix will also be lining its pockets here anyway. It is set to receive a $2.8 billion breakup fee from WBD for ending the existing merger agreement, with Paramount agreeing in its revised offer to cover that cost. In addition, Netflix avoids taking on approximately $60 billion in acquisition-related and studio and streaming net debt tied to the transaction. “Net-net,” Blackledge summed up, “we view Netflix as well positioned to continue to drive viewership share gains both in the US and globally amid the transition to on-demand, streaming TV.”

Bottom line, Blackledge reiterated a Buy rating on the shares along with a $112 price target, a figure that points toward 12-month returns of 21%. (To watch Blackledge’s track record, click here)

Elsewhere on the Street, the stock claims an additional 27 Buys, 8 Holds and 1 Sell, for a Moderate Buy consensus rating. At $114.55, the average target implies shares will climb 24% in the year ahead. (See NFLX stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: A Unique Company Offering an ‘Attractive Entry Point,’ Says Rosenblatt About Palantir Stock
data.description:
“N of 1” is a term Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) CEO Alex Karp uses to describe the company as a unique player in the AI software market with no direct competitors.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

It’s a bold, almost arrogant way of saying they see themselves as totally unmatched, a way of signaling that, in Karp’s view, no other company delivers comparable value.

Is it justified? Rosenblatt analyst John McPeake seems to think so. “Yes, Palantir Really Is an ‘N of 1’… With a “rule of 40” (4Q revenue growth of 70% plus adjusted operating margin of 57%) of 127% and 720bp of rev growth acceleration in 4Q, Palantir is indeed the ‘N of 1’ that CEO Alex Karp claims,” the analyst said.  

Palantir blends its proprietary infrastructure software with “best-in-class technology practitioners” to deliver fast, high-value solutions for enterprises and governments worldwide. McPeake says he has seen the company grow from a loss-making, services-focused startup in the early 2000s into a software leader with repeatable solutions, now generating 84% gross margins and 51% operating margins. “In enterprise software, we think Palantir will add more free cash flow dollars in 2026 than any software company except for SAP,” McPeake further added. “We think Palantir is currently the only stack-agnostic company in technology that can provide an integrated enterprise platform for the rapid deployment of AI.”

Palantir’s momentum has been driven by close partnerships with governments and defense agencies, along with fast, high-value deployments at major global enterprises, including the U.S. DoD, the CIA, BP, Merck, and General Mills. McPeake’s conversations with several customers suggest there is still significant expansion potential within their existing base. The company’s “biggest moat” lies in the synergy between its “polymathic engineering team” and its extensive, mission-critical, battle-tested infrastructure and integration software. With strong demand from both traditional IT modernization and emerging enterprise AI projects, McPeake sees Palantir as well-positioned to achieve double-digit top- and bottom-line growth over the next decade.

Famously, Palantir’s valuation is often mentioned as a turn-off for some. Indeed, a review of the biggest wealth-generating technology companies since 1981 shows that, even with its trailing twelve-month price-to-sales ratio dropping from a peak of 153x to 75x, Palantir remains an outlier.

But McPeake thinks the valuation is justified. “If one looks at the companies that have created the most value in technology when they were a similar size to Palantir, none of them was able to grow as fast as Palantir is forecasted to grow in year 8 (equivalent to Palantir’s guidance in 2026),” the analyst explained. Moreover, at least two of those companies – Facebook and Google – relied on major transformative acquisitions midway through that period, while Palantir’s growth has been entirely organic.

Given the stock has declined by 34.4% from last year’s highs, McPeake thinks the shares now offer an “attractive entry point.” As such, McPeake initiated coverage of PLTR stock with a Buy rating, backed by a $150 price target. Should the figure be met, investors will be pocketing returns of 10% a year from now. (To watch McPeake’s track record, click here)

The Street’s average target is actually a more exuberant $188.82, a figure that points toward 12-month gains of 39%. On the rating front, based on a mix of 14 Buys, 4 Holds and 2 Sells, the stock claims a Moderate Buy consensus rating. (See Palantir stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Keep On Buying Broadcom Shares, Says Oppenheimer Ahead of F1Q Results
data.description:
Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) will finally step up to the earnings plate next week, with the chip giant slated to release its fiscal first quarter (January) results on Wednesday (March 4).

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

As was evident in the market’s downbeat reaction to Nvidia’s outstanding report, leading chipmakers are out of favor right now, even when putting on estimate-trouncing performances.

It remains to be seen whether Broadcom will buck the trend, but in any case, Oppenheimer’s Rick Schafer, an analyst ranked among the top 1% on Wall Street, expects the company to beat Street expectations.

Schafer sees the results exceeding the Street’s F1Q revenue and EPS estimates of $19.2 billion and $2.02, as well as the F2Q (April) forecasts of $20.4 billion and $2.15, driven primarily by AI-related demand.

Schafer sees the Semiconductor segment, which represents 61% of sales, increasing 12% quarter-over-quarter and 51% year-over-year. AI-related revenue is expected to rise 27% sequentially and 103% YoY.  Non-AI semiconductors, which account for 42% of the Semiconductor segment, are projected to decline 11% QoQ and remain flat YoY. Wireless trends are seasonally softer, while Apple has shifted to its in-house N1 wireless chip across all iPhone 17 SKUs. For the upcoming iPhone 18 cycle, Schafer believes Broadcom will take over the UHB PAD component previously supplied by Qorvo, representing a share gain for the company. Additionally, a potential foldable iPhone with Touch ID could increase Broadcom’s chip content per device.

Software, which accounts for 39% of the mix, is expected to drop 2% QoQ and increase 1% YoY, with growth of approximately 13% anticipated in FY26. Schafer believes infrastructure and security software are less exposed to AI-driven disruption compared with application software peers. VMware (infrastructure), Symantec (security), and CA Technologies (infrastructure) provide mission-critical core software that is deeply embedded within enterprise IT environments, resulting in high switching costs for customers.

Schafer sees potential upside to his $50 billion AI revenue estimate for FY26 as additional purchase orders come through. The ASIC customer base includes Alphabet, Meta Platforms, TikTok U.S., Anthropic, and a fifth unnamed customer generating more than $1 billion. Key customer Alphabet could deploy 3.5 million TPU units this year, with the potential to double that volume in CY27, while TPU deployments are also broadening to support large language models beyond Google’s internal workloads. Anthropic, already a $10 billion TPU customer, has committed an additional $11 billion for the second half of 2026.

“AVGO boasts the No. 2 AI franchise (after NVDA), leading the market in AI ASIC with seven projects announced,” the 5-star analyst summed up. “Sustainable growth and cash return supported by core AI ASIC/networking, wireless, broadband, server/storage, and software franchises. We remain long-term buyers.”

Schafer’s Outperform (i.e., Buy) is backed by a $450 price target, implying the stock will gain 40% over the coming months. (To watch Schafer’s track record, click here)

The Street’s average target is almost the same as Schafer’s; at $452.32, the figure makes room for 12-month returns of 41%. All told, based on a mix of 28 Buys and 2 Holds, the analyst consensus rates the stock a Strong Buy. (See AVGO stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: 2 ‘Perfect 10’ Stocks the Street Thinks You Shouldn’t Ignore
data.description:
Sometimes, it seems that the markets don’t know what to do. We’re seeing gains one day, losses the next, and then more gains. The Q4 earnings have been solid, which is always good to see, and while GDP has slowed, it remains positive. We’re still waiting to see how the ripples will spread from the Supreme Court’s decision on President Trump’s tariff policies.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

In all, it’s a macro picture that gives support to both the bulls and the bears – and at times like this, what investors really need is a clear sign, a signal that this stock is the one to buy, and that stock is the one to avoid. Fortunately, there are plenty of such signals to choose from.

One of the best comes from the Smart Score, an AI-powered data tool from TipRanks. The Smart Score gathers and collates the volumes of raw information put out by the markets every day – and puts that information to work. It uses that data to rate every stock in the public markets – and give each one a score, based on a set of factors that is known to correlate with future outperformance. The score is set on a simple, intuitive scale of 1 to 10 – with the ‘Perfect 10’ indicating stocks that the data says are solid.

It’s a good signal. And when Wall Street’s analysts are in-line with it, putting their own ‘Buy’ recommendations on Perfect 10 stocks, that is a dual signal that should definitely be more compelling to discerning investors.

So let’s do just this. We’ve used the TipRanks database to pull up the details on two ‘Perfect 10’ stocks that are also getting high ratings from the analysts. Here they are.

Accenture (ACN)

The first ‘Perfect 10’ on today’s list is Accenture, an IT services and consulting company with a global footprint and a strong bent toward AI and data sciences. Accenture offers business and enterprise clients the services they need to reinvent the ways they remain relevant to customers. From cloud and cybersecurity to AI and data, to marketing, learning, and finance – Accenture can build all of these, and more.

To do this, Accenture brings together the combined expertise of a large, worldwide workforce, over 780,000 strong, and matches it with proprietary tech platforms and assets, and deep knowledge of the full ecosystem in today’s corporate world and its combination of digital tech and business.

Accenture, with its $121 billion market cap and global reach, has the resources to become the ‘reinvention partner of choice’ for some of today’s biggest business players. The company’s partners include such major names as SAP, Microsoft, AWS, Oracle, and Salesforce.

The company has found great success in this approach, and that can be seen from its fiscal 2025 results. In that fiscal year, the last with full data on record, Accenture devoted $3.3 billion to ventures & acquisitions, research & development, and learning & development; in all, it was a large investment in the company’s own future. In addition, Accenture brought in $69.7 billion in annual revenue and returned $8.3 billion in cash to shareholders, mainly through its common-stock dividend.

In December, Accenture reported its results for fiscal 1Q26. The company generated $18.7 billion in revenue, for a 6% year-over-year increase, and beat the forecast by over $210 million. At the bottom line, the company’s non-GAAP EPS of $3.94 was up 10% year-over-year and was 22 cents per share better than expected.

Morgan Stanley’s James Faucette, in covering this stock, notes that most of any headwinds are already accounted for – and that Accenture has a sound path forward. He writes, “We think 1) market fears around potential Gen AI disruption and 2) DOGE impact are now largely baked into ACN’s current valuation at 19x FY27 EPS; further, we think M&A and ecosystem partnerships are growth levers. We believe estimates have likely bottomed and that future revisions are skewed to the upside, driven by lower interest rates and more industry clarity on AI investment path. Our growth estimates (FY26: +5%; FY27: +7% ) sit well above consensus growth figures… With a 4.5:1 Bull-Bear skew, we find ACN compelling at current valuation levels (~13% discount to S&P 500).”

Faucette’s Overweight (Buy) rating comes with a $320 price target that points to a one-year gain for the stock of 54%. (To watch Faucette’s track record, click here.)

Overall, these shares get a Moderate Buy rating from the Street’s analyst consensus, based on 17 reviews that include 12 Buys and 5 Holds. The stock is selling for $207.38 right now, and its $293.60 average target price implies a 41.5% upside by this time next year. (See ACN stock forecast.)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: ‘Heading Much Lower’: Investor Sees More Downside Ahead for XRP
data.description:
People often bemoan the fact cryptocurrencies offer little real-world use. But that is not a problem XRP (XRP-USD) faces. The token was created to function as a bridge currency within the Ripple Payments network, enabling banks to process cross-border transfers instantly and at relatively low cost.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Last year, XRP reached a fresh all-time high, climbing to $3.65 per token in July. Since then, though, it has dropped 60.5% as part of the wider downturn across the crypto market.

The question now is whether its role in facilitating cross-border payments sets the scene for a comeback.

Investor Anthony Di Pizio is skeptical that will be the case. “Although XRP’s use-case should, in theory, drive long-term upside, it’s facing some structural issues that might be difficult to overcome,” said the investor. “If history is any guide, these headwinds might fuel even more downside instead.”

Cross-border bank transfers are often slow and expensive, largely because institutions rely on different systems, including the SWIFT (Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication), which can require intermediaries to complete a transaction. Ripple Payments was designed to address that inefficiency by enabling banks to communicate and settle directly, making transfers near-instant and low cost. XRP was introduced as a bridge currency within the network, allowing, for example, a U.S. bank to send XRP instead of dollars to avoid foreign exchange friction. Transaction costs are negligible, typically just 0.00001 tokens, or a fraction of $0.01.

However, XRP’s role is not essential. Ripple Payments also supports fiat transfers, meaning network growth does not automatically translate into higher demand for XRP. “That’s a key reason why it tends to decline sharply during selloffs in the broader crypto markets – its performance is still very much determined by speculative investors,” Di Pizio explained.

Banks also tend not to hold bridge currencies, immediately converting them into local currency, and XRP’s volatility adds balance sheet risk. That dynamic became more pronounced after Ripple introduced its stablecoin, Ripple USD, in late 2024. With minimal price fluctuation, Ripple USD offers a more stable alternative for institutions, potentially reducing the need for XRP in cross-border transactions.

Prior to last year’s high, XRP’s previous peak came in 2018, after which the token lost more than 90% of its value within months and then spent most of the following five years below $1. Di Pizio thinks a “similar outcome is in progress right now.”

Because Ripple Payments does not generate steady, structural demand for XRP, its price remains heavily influenced by speculative trading. Unless the token develops a compelling new use case that drives consistent adoption, it is likely to remain vulnerable to sharp selloffs during periods of broader crypto market uncertainty, limiting its prospects for “long-term value creation.”

With that in mind, Di Pizio’s forecast is a downbeat one. “If we simply follow history, I think XRP will be trading at somewhere between $0.30 and $0.50 five years from now,” the investor summed up. “That assumes its 2025 high remains the peak for the foreseeable future, and that the decline reaches a similar magnitude to the 2018 decline with no meaningful recovery thereafter.” (To watch Di Pizio’s track record, click here)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

It’s all about triple-digit revenue growth at the rapidly expanding AI cloud infrastructure specialist, and that metric will be a focus for Wall Street. Revenue is anticipated to increase by 101% to $1.5 billion.

However, Evercore’s Amit Daryanani, an analyst ranked among the top 2% on Wall Street, believes the company can beat that figure given what he views as a “de-risked setup.” Recall that in the prior quarter management reduced its CY25 revenue guide to account for temporary delays in delivering powered-shell data center capacity in Q4.

He also sees potential to outperform the Street’s $138 million EBIT forecast, though EPS may be harder to model accurately as CRWV continues investing to scale operations.

For the December quarter, Daryanani thinks investor attention will center on the schedule for powered-shell data center deliveries, progress in locking in incremental long-term capacity and the funding environment – with the broadened NVIDIA partnership viewed as a supportive factor – as well as the read-through for CY26 revenue.

Recent reporting referencing internal memos about a “chip delivery milestone” at a facility in Denton, Texas, indicated that more than 16,000 GPUs were operational by late December and that total data center capacity could approach roughly 850 MW by year-end. Against that backdrop, the analyst thinks the Q1 Street revenue forecast of about $2.3 billion appears attainable. Daryanani sees the Q4 backlog landing in the mid-to-high $50 billion range, largely unchanged from Q3, as the company prioritizes securing additional data center capacity, with revenue primarily constrained by supply rather than demand.

Additional topics investors will be monitoring include the transition from Blackwell to Rubin and how the ramp timing could affect revenue; availability of powered-shell data center capacity, including updates on self-built facilities; and the strategy to generate revenue from software offerings such as SUNK and Mission Control by selling them to other cloud providers. Overall, while strong demand and the debate over GPU useful life – with several data points suggesting GPUs can remain productive for more than 10 years – are well understood, the 5-star analyst thinks attention has increasingly shifted toward CRWV’s ability to secure the capacity required to sustain future expansion.

All told, Daryanani maintained an Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares, backed by a $160 price target that points toward 12-month returns of 65%. (To watch Daryanani’s track record, click here)

The rest of the Street’s ratings splits into an even 8 Buys and Holds, each, all culminating in a Moderate Buy consensus rating. At $118.57, the average target implies shares will gain 22% over the one-year timeframe. (See CRWV stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: AI Companions Could Make Apple Stock an AI Winner, Says J.P. Morgan
data.description:
We’ve got Generative AI, Agentic AI, Embodied AI, Multimodal AI, to name but a few AI combos. You can also add AI Companions to the list, and the one company that stands to benefit from these is the one tech giant yet to fully get its AI game in order. That would be Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), of course, often accused of being an AI laggard with no well-defined AI strategy.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

However, J.P. Morgan’s Samik Chatterjee, an analyst ranked among the top 1% on Wall Street, thinks the company is well-positioned to make the most of this opportunity.

Consumer technology is in the early stages of a “structural shift” toward AI-native personal companion devices. According to several press reports, Apple is developing several AI companion form factors, including smart glasses, wearable AI pins, and smart home assistants. “We view a potential foray into AI Companions to be important for Apple on both defensive and offensive fronts, to firstly defend its wide moat in Personal Computing devices, and secondly to position itself better in the new category of Personal devices – AI Companions,” the 5-star analyst said.

Chatterjee thinks Apple’s long-standing strengths, including its tightly integrated product ecosystem and “uncompromised focus” on privacy, are likely to remain key draws for consumers adopting personal AI Companions. The company is well placed to build on and expand what these companions can do, tapping into the power of its other devices – not just the wearables – to handle heavier AI processing, while also enabling seamless access to a broad range of services that are already in use. Over time, those services should incorporate additional AI features and connect smoothly with the new Companion devices.

Chatterjee believes that the launch of a personalized Siri, capable of maintaining “conversational context” on Apple’s AI Companions, will be a key factor in their potential success. This includes how well the system integrates with apps and leverages multimodal inputs across voice, text, and images. Moreover, embedding AI functionality with context drawn from the user’s existing devices and native apps – such as email, notes, messages, contacts, and Safari – could be a major differentiator, giving Apple’s companions an edge over standalone devices from third-party providers. “We believe that launch of personalized Siri is highly likely in 2026, particularly given recent partnership with Google to leverage the foundational Gemini models, and while AI Companion devices are likely to be highlighted in 2026, we expect a more material volume ramp starting in 2027,” Chatterjee further expounded.

Looking at volume and revenue, Chatterjee forecasts Apple’s AI Companions reaching 50 million units across the full range of devices by the end of the decade, generating roughly $25 billion in hardware revenue alone. Expanding adoption beyond Apple’s current installed base could also open up opportunities for standalone services monetization. To put this in perspective, $25 billion is more than two-thirds of the present revenue from the Wearables business. When combining potential hardware and services profits, Chatterjee estimates these AI companions could contribute around 5% to Apple’s EPS by FY30.

All told, Chatterjee reiterated an Overweight (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares, backed by a $325 price target. Should the figure be met, investors will be pocketing returns of 20% a year from now. (To watch Chatterjee’s track record, click here)

16 other analysts join Chatterjee in the bull camp while an additional 9 Holds and 1 Sell add up to a Moderate Buy consensus rating. The forecast calls for 12-month gains of 13%, considering the average target clocks in at $307.55. (See Apple stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel
